sit: A shooter is on a breakaway and dekes hard to their backhand. You start to go with them. They pull back to their forehand. What do you do?
opts: Commit fully to the backhand side since you already started — trust your first read and drop into a butterfly over there
Stop your feet immediately and try to reset — don't fully commit to the fake
Dive across the crease flat on your pads to cover both posts at once
Skate further out to challenge

sit: The puck carrier is tight to the right side, almost at the goal line. You've moved to your right post. They cut hard toward the middle. What do you do?
opts: Stay tight on the post and trust it — the post takes away the short side so don't move even if the shooter cuts across the crease area on you
Push off the post and move with them — they've changed the angle and you need to stay square to the puck
Drop into a butterfly on the post — seal the ice along the post and take away the low shot since they're cutting toward the middle from the goal line there
Come way out into the crease to challenge — step out aggressively to cut down the angle as they cut across the front of the net to shoot

sit: Your two defensemen are both going to the corner to battle for a puck. An attacker is cutting to the front of the net with nobody covering them. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing — it's a D problem
Call out loudly to your nearest forward — 'Front! Front!' — so they know to pick up the open player
Skate out of your crease to cover the open attacker in front of the net yourself
Drop into your butterfly early and wait for the pass to come out of the corner

sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. The power play passes it from the low corner up to the point quickly. You were set for the low play. What do you have to do?
opts: Stay in your current low position — you're already set for a shot, so trust your positioning and let the point shooter try to beat you from distance
Move out to the top of your crease immediately — the point shot requires a different position than the low play
Drop into butterfly right away — a point shot usually goes low with traffic in front, so get your pads down early to take away the bottom
Back up to your goal line and square up — giving yourself more time to see the shot is the best play when the puck moves up to the point

sit: You're down and out of position. The puck is sliding toward an open corner of the net and you can't get up in time. What do you try?
opts: Give up the goal and reset — once you're down you can't recover, so accept it and get back in position for the next shift after the puck drop
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction
Call for a penalty on the shooter — wave your glove at the ref because you were knocked down and ask for a delayed call on the play
Stay still and trust the post — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing in place is the best chance to get lucky

sit: You're set on the right post. The puck is on the right half-wall. A player cuts backdoor to the left post — completely open. The pass is about to happen. What do you do?
opts: Stay on the right post and trust your teammates — your D should take the backdoor player so hold your post and take away the half-wall shooter
T-push hard across to the left post — the backdoor player is the immediate danger, the half-wall shooter is not
Come out of your crease toward the puck carrier on the half-wall — challenge the shooter and take away the angle since they have the puck right now
Drop into a butterfly on the right post — seal the ice on the short side and let your defenseman take the backdoor player cutting to the far post

sit: A shooter is coming down the right wing at full speed. Before they shoot you notice their elbow is dropping low. What does this usually mean?
opts: Nothing — just random body language — shooters move their arms in all kinds of ways before a shot and there's no real pattern you can read off it
They're likely shooting high — a dropping elbow often indicates a high release
They're going to pass instead — a dropping elbow usually means the shooter is about to sauce a pass across to a teammate on the weak side
They'll deke first before shooting — dropping the elbow is how shooters load up for a deke move to the forehand before they put it in the net

sit: A shooter has the puck in tight and is trying to shoot between your legs. What's the best position to take away the 5-hole?
opts: Spread your legs wide apart in a stance — giving yourself a solid base takes away the low corners of the net and keeps you balanced on the ice
Keep your pads together or slightly overlapping and squeeze your knees — make the 5-hole as small as possible
Drop into a butterfly immediately on the ice — getting your pads flat on the ice seals the 5-hole and takes away any low shot between your legs
Stand with your feet at shoulder width for balance — a normal stance gives you the mobility to react to any shot, including through the 5-hole

sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You make a save and the puck is loose at your feet. Your team has it covered nearby. Should you freeze it?
opts: Freeze the puck immediately to kill the clock — any stoppage you can get late in a one-goal game is good because it burns time off the clock
Leave it for your team — freezing it gives the other team a faceoff in your zone with 90 seconds left, which is dangerous
Kick the puck away from the front of your net — send it anywhere away from the danger area and let your team sort out possession after
Throw the puck to the corner with your glove — get it out of the slot area and let your defensemen go retrieve it behind the goal line

sit: You can see that both opposing forwards are sneaking behind your defensemen as your D make a D-to-D pass in the offensive zone. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing and let your D handle it
Call out immediately — 'Two coming!' — to warn your D that forwards are breaking behind them
Wait a few seconds to see if your D notice
Yell for a line change so a fresh group can come on and sort the coverage out

sit: An attacker carries the puck behind your net at speed, going from your left to your right. They're about to try a wraparound. What do you do?
opts: Stay in the middle of your crease and wait for the puck to appear on the other side
Move with them — push to the right post as they come around and seal it. Don't let them get the post.
Come out past the crease to stop them
Drop into the butterfly right away and slide across behind you

sit: A player is awarded a penalty shot. They're coming straight at you. They haven't shown you anything yet. What's your strategy?
opts: Skate all the way out to the blue line so you cut their shooting angle down to almost nothing
Come to the top of the crease, stay on your feet as long as possible, and force them to make the first move
Drop to the butterfly as soon as they cross the blue line
Stay planted right on your goal line

sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The other team has the puck. One of your teammates skates to the puck instead of covering their player. What's the mistake?
opts: They should have stayed on the bench longer — too many players out there at once causes confusion, so the fix is a quicker line change on the shift
They left their man open — in the D-zone, everyone needs to have a player covered before chasing the puck
They should have gone straight to the net front — the slot is the most dangerous area in your zone so that's where everyone needs to be
They skated too slow on that play — if they had chased harder they would have won the puck battle and taken possession back for the team

sit: Your teammate carries the puck into the offensive zone. You're skating hard to join the rush but you cross the blue line before the puck does. What went wrong?
opts: You were skating too slow to keep up with the play — if you had more speed through the neutral zone you would have caught up to the puck
You entered the zone before the puck offside. You need to time your entry so the puck crosses the line first.
You should have stopped at center ice and waited — a trailing forward should stay back at the red line until the puck is clearly in the zone
The puck carrier was too slow getting over the line — if they had pushed the pace harder you wouldn't have gotten ahead of the puck at all

sit: On the forecheck, all three forwards go deep into the corner chasing the puck. The opposing D makes a quick pass up the middle and the other team breaks out with speed. What was the mistake?
opts: The forwards weren't skating hard enough — if all three had chased with more speed they would have caught the puck before it got passed up
All three forwards went deep — at least one forward needs to stay high to prevent the breakout pass through the middle
The defensemen were too far back at the blue line — they should have stepped up to pinch and kept the puck in the offensive zone right away
The goalie didn't stop the breakout pass across the ice — goalies should play the puck when their team is forechecking hard in the offensive zone

sit: You're the D at the offensive blue line. The puck is loose along the boards. You pinch down to get it, but the opposing forward gets there first and goes around you for a breakaway. What was the mistake?
opts: You should have pinched harder and finished the check — if you commit to a pinch you have to follow through, so a half pinch is what got beat on the play
You pinched when the puck wasn't clearly yours to win — if you can't get there first, hold the line instead of gambling
Your partner should have covered for you — when one D pinches, the other D rotates to the middle, and your partner didn't read the play in time there
The forward got lucky on the bounce — the puck took a weird bounce off the boards that nobody could have predicted during that battle

sit: Your team loses the puck in the offensive zone. Two forwards stand and watch for a moment before skating back. The other team gets a 3-on-2 rush. What's the mistake?
opts: The defensemen were out of position when the turnover happened — they were too deep in the offensive zone and couldn't get back in time at all
The forwards didn't transition immediately — when you lose the puck, you turn and skate back right away, no watching
The goalie came out too far from the net — he was too aggressive playing the puck and couldn't recover in time to stop the three-on-two rush
The coach called the wrong play on that shift — the system your team was running left too many forwards deep in the offensive zone area

sit: During a line change, the fresh player jumps on the ice while the tired player is still at center ice skating slowly to the bench. The ref blows the whistle. What happened?
opts: The ref made a bad call on the change — the refs often miss line changes at this level and this one clearly shouldn't have been blown down at all
Too many men on the ice — the fresh player jumped on before the tired player was close enough to the bench. The outgoing player needs to be within arm's reach of the bench.
Offside at center — the fresh player jumped onto the ice past the red line while the puck was still in the defensive zone, so the play was ruled offside
Icing on the tired player — the tired player skated past center without playing the puck so the ref blew it down for icing against the team that was changing

sit: You pick up the puck along the boards. A defender is coming from your right side. You turn to your right — directly into the defender — and lose the puck. What should you have done?
opts: Turned faster on the turn — if you had whipped your body around quicker the defender wouldn't have caught up and you'd have kept possession
Turned away from the pressure — to your left — protecting the puck with your body between you and the defender
Shot the puck immediately off the boards — a quick wrist shot toward the net would have been better than trying to turn into a defender
Called for help louder from your teammates — a quick yell brings a winger low to support you along the boards before you get hit

sit: Your first forechecker pressures the D behind the net. The D panics and tries a blind backhand pass up the middle. What should happen next?
opts: Your team backs off and resets — let the other team complete the pass and set up your defensive structure at your own blue line with gap control
Your second forward, who stayed high, intercepts the pass in the slot exactly why they stayed high
Everyone goes to the corner to support — all three forwards collapse low to pin the puck against the boards and win back possession of the play
The play resets at center ice — the blind pass usually gets knocked into neutral ice and both teams skate back to their benches for a new cycle

sit: You've steered the puck carrier to the outside with good gap control. Suddenly they cut hard back to the inside. What happens next?
opts: You keep skating backward to the outside and let them cut — stay in your lane and trust your positioning, since forcing outside is still the goal
You pivot and match their cut — stay on their inside hip and use your body to close them off
You stop and watch the cut — let them cross the middle so you can see where they're going before you commit to a defensive move
You go for a big hip check — when they cut back inside they expose themselves to a check, so step up and knock them off the puck quick

sit: Your team forces a turnover at your own blue line. The other team's forwards are all deep in your zone. Your winger has the puck with open ice ahead. What happens next?
opts: They dump the puck in and change lines — dumping it deep is the safe play after a turnover so your tired players can change for fresh legs
They attack with speed — the other team is caught deep, so this is a rush chance. Go now before they recover.
They pass the puck back to the goalie to reset — starting a controlled breakout is better than rushing into a broken play with no support up ice
They wait for everyone to get set in position — regrouping at center ice gives your team a full five-player attack into the offensive zone

sit: On the power play, the puck is on the left half-wall. The PK is overloading that side. Your teammate makes a sharp cross-ice pass to the right side where a player is wide open. What happens next?
opts: The open player passes the puck back across — they send it back to the left half-wall to reset the power play because they don't have a clean shot yet
The open player has a one-timer or clean shot — the PK can't recover across the ice fast enough, so this is a prime scoring chance
The open player skates it to the corner — they carry the puck down low behind the goal line to set up a cycle with the forwards on that side
The play dies — the PK recovers too fast so the open player just holds the puck and waits for everyone to set up in their positions again

sit: Your team is under heavy pressure in the D-zone. A defenseman panics and shoots the puck all the way down the ice from behind the red line. No one touches it. What happens next?
opts: Play continues normally — the puck came from behind the red line but nobody touched it, so the ref lets the play go and the other team gets possession
The ref blows the whistle for icing — faceoff comes back to your zone and your tired players can't change
Your team gets a power play on the shot — shooting the puck the length of the ice under pressure is legal, and the panic clear gets ruled clean
The other team gets a minor penalty — a defenseman panicking and firing the puck down is a delay of game, so the refs call two minutes on it

sit: You're battling for the puck along the boards in the offensive zone. You get lower than the defender and pin the puck with your skate. Your linemate is calling for it. What happens next?
opts: You hold the puck forever on your skate — keeping it pinned kills time, tires the defender, and eventually the ref will blow the whistle for a stoppage in play
You kick the puck to your stick and make a quick pass to your linemate before the next defender arrives — you won the battle, now use it
You leave the puck and skate away — once you've tied up the defender your job is done, so release to the slot and let a teammate grab it
You shoot the puck on net right off your skate — any puck toward the net from the boards can cause a rebound that a teammate can tap in quick

sit: You pick up the puck at center ice with a full head of steam. The defender is flat-footed at the blue line. You have a clear speed advantage. What happens next?
opts: You dump the puck deep and chase — a flat-footed D won't react fast, but dumping the puck in is always the safer play than carrying it across the line
You use your speed to carry the puck wide around the flat-footed defender — your speed advantage makes the carry the best option
You stop at the blue line and wait — let the defender commit first, then make your read once your wingers catch up for a full zone entry
You pass it backward to the trailer — drop the puck to your center at center ice and let them attack with fresh speed through the neutral zone
